symptom: Newly installed smart thermostat (Nest, Ecobee, Honeywell T-series) won't power on, reboots repeatedly, loses Wi-Fi, or shows low battery despite being hardwired. Older mechanical or battery stat worked fine on the same wiring. HVAC system may short-cycle randomly.
diagnosis: Missing or unterminated common (C) wire. Smart thermostats need continuous 24VAC to power the display and Wi-Fi radio. Legacy stats ran on batteries or trickle-charged through the call circuit. Without C, the new stat parasitically pulls current and trips the furnace board safety timers.
procedure: Kill power to the furnace/air handler at the service switch AND breaker. The stat is 24V but the transformer primary is 120/240V.
Pull the stat off the wall plate. Photograph and label every conductor before disconnecting.
Count conductors. 4-wire cable (R, W, Y, G) is the classic no-C install. 5+ wire cable usually has an unused conductor pushed into the wall — look for a blue or black wire.
Option A (spare wire): land it on C at the furnace board and on C at the stat. Done.
Option B (no spare, G not needed and stat supports it): repurpose G as C per the manufacturer install guide. Do not guess — read the doc.
Option C (no spare, all wires used): install a power-extender kit (Ecobee PEK, Nest Power Connector) at the furnace, or pull new 18/5 thermostat cable.
Restore power. Verify 24VAC between R and C at the baseplate with a multimeter before mounting.
Mount the stat, run the setup wizard. Run a full heat and cool cycle. No short-cycling in 30 minutes = good.
Document the wire mapping on the back of the plate so the next tech isn't guessing.
safety_notes: Kill 120/240V at the breaker, not just the service switch. Some older installs wire the transformer primary continuous.
Never jumper R to C to 'give it power'. That's a dead short across the 24V transformer secondary and blows the board fuse (typical 3A or 5A ATC blade) in seconds.
If the existing transformer is a 20VA unit, a smart stat plus Wi-Fi can push it over rating. Upgrade to 40VA if nuisance fuse blows repeat.
Dual-transformer systems: Rh and Rc must NOT be jumpered if two transformers are present. You'll short them together.
Heat pump O/B terminal is brand-specific: O = Carrier/Trane cooling-energized, B = Rheem/Ruud heating-energized. Wrong setting = system heats when customer asks for cool.
